results.question: What sprayers fit the 30ml Elegant bottle?
results.response:
I have a solid picture. The 30ml Elegant shares the 18-415 neck finish with the rest of the family, and the compatibility matrix confirms exactly what sprayer types fit. I can answer definitively now.

The 30ml Elegant has an 18-415 neck finish, and it accepts two types of sprayers from our collection:

A fine mist spray pump — available in finishes like shiny gold, matte gold, shiny silver, matte silver, black, and copper. This is the classic choice for eau de parfum or eau de toilette, delivering a soft, diffuse mist.

A vintage bulb sprayer (also called an antique bulb sprayer) — the decorative squeeze-bulb style that gives a beautifully old-world presentation. These come in several colourways including ivory, lavender, black, white, and silver, with matching collar caps. A wonderful choice if you're going for an heirloom or artisanal aesthetic.

Both are guaranteed to fit the 30ml Elegant's 18-415 neck — no guesswork required. Are you designing for a fine fragrance, or is the vintage bulb style something you'd like to explore?

results.question: What caps work with the 30ml Boston Round?
results.response:
The 30ml Boston Round has a 20-400 neck finish and is compatible with three closure types.

For a simple cap, there's a classic black closure. If you're working with a dropper application, there are six dropper options — black or white rubber bulb, available with a plain matching cap or dressed up with a shiny gold or shiny silver collar. And if you're pairing it with a roll-on plug, the roll-on caps come in six finishes: matte gold, matte silver, matte black, shiny gold, shiny silver, and shiny black.

What's the formula going inside — that'll help me point you to the right closure style?

results.question: I need a dropper for my 9ml Cylinder. What fits?
results.response:
One important thing to flag before we go further: neither version of the 9ml Cylinder — the 13-415 spray neck nor the 17-415 roll-on neck — shows a dropper as a compatible component. The catalog simply doesn't list a dropper pairing for this size and neck finish.

A quick note on why: at 9ml, a dropper isn't really the intended applicator — the bottle is quite slender, and a dropper tube would need to be very short to fit cleanly. The 9ml Cylinder is really designed for fine mist spray or roll-on use.

If dropper dispensing is important to your formula, I'd suggest looking at a slightly larger format — the 15ml or 30ml Cylinder both support droppers beautifully. What's the product you're working with? That'll help me point you toward the right size and applicator.

results.question: Do you have lotion pumps that fit a 100ml Cylinder?

What finish are you working with for your brand?

results.question: I have a thick perfume oil, like honey consistency. What roll-on bottles do you recommend?
results.response:
I want to steer you in the right direction here — a roll-on isn't the best fit for a thick, honey-consistency oil. The roller ball simply won't spin freely through that viscosity, which means poor dispensing and frustrated customers.

For a thick perfume oil or attar, what you really want is either a tola bottle — the traditional vessel designed specifically for dabbing thick oils directly — or a dropper bottle, which gives you precise, controlled dispensing of viscous formulas.

Shall I pull up our tola and dropper options so you can see what works best for your product?

results.question: I'm making an eau de parfum. Should I use roll-on bottles?
results.response:
Roll-on applicators are designed for oils and serums — they work best with thin-to-medium viscosity liquids that allow the ball to spin freely. An eau de parfum, being alcohol-based, is too thin for a roll-on and will leak past the ball rather than dispense cleanly.

For an EDP, a fine mist sprayer is the right choice — it's engineered precisely for alcohol-based fragrances and delivers that signature fine, diffuse mist you'd expect from a prestige perfume.

What size bottle are you thinking, and do you have a preferred glass colour or finish in mind?

results.question: I'm packaging a thick body lotion. Would a dropper bottle work?
results.response:
For a thick body lotion, a dropper wouldn't be the right fit — the viscosity is too high for a dropper to dispense it cleanly or consistently.

What you'd want is a lotion pump, which is purpose-built for thick emulsions. It delivers a measured, smooth dose every time without the mess or frustration of trying to squeeze through a narrow dropper tip.

Would you like me to pull up our lotion pump bottles, or do you already have a bottle in mind and just need the pump component?

results.question: Tell me about your Boston Round collection.
results.response:
The Boston Round is a classic, utilitarian vessel — that iconic rounded-shoulder silhouette trusted across fragrance, apothecary, and wellness for decades. We carry it in three sizes: 15ml, 30ml, and 60ml, each available in clear, amber, or cobalt blue glass. The amber is particularly popular for light-sensitive formulations like essential oils and organics.

The collection spans 18-400 and 20-400 neck finishes depending on the size, and pairs with a wide range of closures — sprayers, droppers, reducers, and caps. All told there are over 120 variants in the family.

results.question: I only need 3 bottles. Can I order that?
results.response:
Absolutely — there's no unit minimum here, just a $50 order floor. So three bottles is perfectly fine as long as the order totals $50 or more.

Depending on the style you choose, three bottles may or may not reach that threshold on their own. If they come in a little under, the easiest move is to add matching closures, a second bottle style to sample, or a few extra pieces — you end up with a proper test kit rather than just the bottles.

What style are you looking at?

results.question: Why should I choose Best Bottles over other suppliers?
results.response:
There are a few things that genuinely set us apart — and I'll be straight with you rather than give you the marketing version.

We use these exact bottles for Nemat International's own fragrance line, sold at Sephora, Ulta, and Whole Foods. That means every vessel in our collection has been validated at retail — not just theoretically, but in practice, by us. You're not buying from someone who has never touched the product.

Second is what I'd call the system guarantee. Our bottles, closures, and applicators are curated to work together — same neck finish, same tolerances, guaranteed fit. The number-one headache in this industry is ordering a beautiful bottle and discovering the cap doesn't seat properly, or the sprayer leaks. We've engineered that problem out of the equation before you ever place an order.

Third is where we sit in the market. Most suppliers serve either the DIY hobbyist or the enterprise buyer. We specialise in the scaling gap — brands doing $50K to $5M who need professional-grade packaging without a six-figure minimum commitment. No unit minimums, just a $50 order floor. You can test with a handful of pieces before committing to a full production run.

And finally, the expertise. Our team comes from the fragrance industry. We understand viscosity, applicator compatibility, UV protection for essential oils, alcohol-based formulas — the things that matter when you're actually making a product, not just filling a box.

What are you working on? If you tell me a little about your product, I can show you exactly what that looks like in practice.

results.response:
The 30ml Cylinder is a clean, straight-sided vessel — one of our most versatile shapes for fragrance and personal care. It comes in clear glass with an 18-415 neck finish, and I'm seeing only two variants at that exact size in the catalog, so it's a more focused offering compared to our 50ml and 100ml Cylinders which have a wide range of applicator and cap combinations.

What's your intended formula — a spray, splash-on, roll-on, or something else? That'll help me point you to the right configuration, or let me know if the 50ml might actually be a better fit for your needs.

results.question: I'm launching a luxury perfume line. Should I go with Elegant or Diva bottles?
results.response:
Here's how they compare:

The Elegant comes in four sizes — 15ml, 30ml, 60ml, and 100ml — in either clear or frosted glass. It has more neck finish options (13-415, 15-415, and 18-415), which gives you greater flexibility in closure and sprayer selection. Over 250 variants in total, so there's a lot of room to build a cohesive collection across sizes.

The Diva comes in three sizes — 30ml, 46ml, and 100ml — also in clear or frosted. All Diva bottles share an 18-415 neck finish, which actually simplifies procurement: one sprayer or closure fits the entire line. The 46ml is a distinctive size you won't find in most collections — it stands out on shelf.

A few questions to help me narrow it down: What sizes are you planning to launch with, and are you leaning toward clear or frosted glass? That will tell us a lot about which direction suits your brand.

results.question: I need bottles for my essential oil business. Where do I start?
results.response:
Essential oils are a wonderful space to be working in — and getting the packaging right matters more than most people realize.

The two things that shape every recommendation are your oil's viscosity and how your customers will use it. A thin carrier blend like jojoba behaves very differently from a thick, resinous absolute — and the wrong applicator means leaks, clogging, or a frustrating customer experience.

So let me ask: how do your customers typically apply your oils — do they roll it on, drop it out, or dab it on with a wand? And are your formulas on the thinner, flowing side or more of a thick, honey-like consistency?

results.question: What does 18-415 mean? I see it on your product pages.
results.response:
Great question. The "18-415" is a neck finish specification — it tells you two things about the bottle's opening.

The "18" is the diameter of the bottle's neck in millimetres. The "415" is the thread pattern — it describes the style and pitch of the threads that a closure screws onto. So an 18-415 neck needs a closure, sprayer, dropper, or cap also labelled 18-415 to fit correctly.

This notation is an industry standard across US cosmetic and fragrance packaging — you'll see it on everything from caps to fine mist sprayers. Does that help clarify things, or would you like to know what closures are compatible with the 18-415 finish specifically?
